label: Central Limit Theorem
definition: The result that the sum or average of many independent random variables tends toward a Gaussian regardless of the original distributions, which is why the bell curve appears so universally. It also fixes the rate at which uncertainty shrinks, like one over the square root of the sample size, so quadrupling your data only halves your error.

body.text: The central limit theorem is one of the most consequential results in all of probability, and it explains a mystery: why the Gaussian appears everywhere. It says that if you add up many independent random variables, each with finite variance, the distribution of their sum, suitably centered and scaled, converges to a Gaussian distribution, no matter what the individual variables looked like. The components can be skewed, discrete, or wildly non-Gaussian; their normalized average still tends toward the same smooth bell curve. The bell curve is not one option among many but the default shape that emerges whenever many small independent effects accumulate.

The precise statement concerns the standardized sum: subtract the mean and divide by the standard deviation, which itself shrinks like one over the square root of the number of terms. Two results are worth separating. The law of large numbers says the sample average converges to the true mean. The central limit theorem says more: it describes the precise shape and rate of the residual fluctuations around that mean, fluctuations that shrink at a rate proportional to one over the square root of the sample size. That square-root rate has a sobering practical consequence: error bars narrow slowly, so quadrupling your data only halves your uncertainty, which is why getting twice as confident costs four times as much data.

The theorem matters because it underwrites a vast amount of statistical practice and machine learning intuition. Confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and the standard error of an estimate all rest on the approximate normality it guarantees for large samples. The assumption of Gaussian noise in linear regression and many probabilistic models is often justified by appeal to it: measurement error is a sum of many small independent perturbations, so it should be roughly normal. Monte Carlo estimates inherit Gaussian error bars from the same source, which is how the precision of a simulated expectation is quantified.

It is equally important to know where the theorem does not apply, because its failures explain real phenomena. The classical version requires finite variance; distributions with heavy tails, such as those governing financial crashes or some natural-language statistics, can violate it and produce sums that remain heavy-tailed and converge instead to other stable laws, which is why rare extreme events are far more common in those domains than a Gaussian would ever predict. Strong dependence between the variables also breaks the result. Understanding these boundaries is what separates a naive assumption of normality from a justified one, and it is why a careful practitioner checks tail behavior and independence before leaning on the central limit theorem.

image.alt: Panels showing the distribution of the sum of n dice for n from 1 to 5: a flat bar chart for one die that grows more bell-shaped as more dice are added, with a final panel overlaying the curves against a normal.
image.caption: The central limit theorem says sums of many independent variables tend toward a Gaussian. A single die is flat and uniform, but the distribution of the sum of several dice grows into the familiar bell curve as their number increases.
